title: Deep audit: 4 bugs fixed in economic_surprise, liquidity_risk, drawdown_analyzer, retirement_mc

bugs_found: economic_surprise.py: CPI surprise normalization always saturated at +/-1.0 — use_change indicators compute relative surprise against tiny MoM % changes (~0.2%), producing values >100% that always clip to the limit, losing all granularity

liquidity_risk.py: truthiness checks on numeric values (0.0 treated as None) — 'if current_amihud' is False when amihud=0.0 (valid for mega-caps), causing display of None instead of 0.0 for amihud_illiquidity, roll_spread_bps, and turnover fields
drawdown_analyzer.py: rolling max drawdown off-by-one — slice prices.iloc[max(0, i-window):i+1] has window+1 elements instead of window, slightly overstating the lookback period
retirement_mc.py: dead rng variable — compute_safe_withdrawal_rate creates rng=np.random.default_rng(seed) but never uses it (inner_rng is used instead)
observation.gap_identified: Multiple service files had subtle correctness bugs affecting data quality and display accuracy

bugs_fixed: economic_surprise.py: Use wider normalization denominator (100.0) for use_change indicators vs 10.0 for level indicators — CPI now produces meaningful intermediate surprise values instead of always +/-1.0
liquidity_risk.py: Changed 4 truthiness checks from 'if x' to 'if x is not None' for amihud_illiquidity, roll_spread_bps, daily_turnover_pct; changed shares_outstanding check to 'if shares_out > 0'
drawdown_analyzer.py: Fixed slice to prices.iloc[i - window + 1:i + 1] giving exactly window elements
retirement_mc.py: Removed unused rng = np.random.default_rng(seed) line

limitations: CPI normalization fix uses a fixed 100.0 denominator — a z-score approach based on historical surprise distribution would be more principled
The drawdown off-by-one is minor (253 vs 252 days) — unlikely to cause material differences in practice
Did not audit all 55 services — focused on 8 files with complex math
assessment.self_critique: The CPI bug was the most impactful find — it systematically biases the economic surprise composite index. Could have also audited monte_carlo.py and crash_model.py for deeper mathematical bugs.

next_steps: Audit crash_model.py and signal_engine.py for similar normalization or clipping issues
Consider z-score normalization for all economic surprise indicators (not just use_change)
Add integration test that verifies the liquidity score for a known mega-cap like AAPL is >80
Audit all service files for truthiness checks on numeric values (0, 0.0, False)
